paragraphs.text:
SRD‑46 shows **two distinct mercury complexation landscapes**: a much richer **Hg(II)** chemistry and a smaller **Hg(I)** set.

| Oxidation state | SRD-46 identifier | Catalog coverage | General picture |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Hg(II) | metal_71 | 20 ligand systems, 92 species | Broad and diverse coordination chemistry |
| Hg(I) | metal_72 | smaller set | More limited coverage; many entries involve halides and simple anions |

claims.text: SRD‑46 shows **two distinct mercury complexation landscapes**
| Hg(II) | metal_71 | 20 ligand systems, 92 species | Broad and diverse coordination chemistry |
| Hg(I) | metal_72 | smaller set | More limited coverage; many entries involve halides and simple anions |

paragraphs.text:
## Hg(II): the dominant complex-forming state
The Hg(II) catalog includes halides, pseudohalides, hydroxide, sulfide-related ligands, neutral N-donors, soft S-donors, and chelators.

Representative Hg(II) ligand families seen in SRD‑46:

| Ligand class | Examples present in SRD‑46 |
|---|---|
| Halides / pseudohalides | Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻, CN⁻/HCN, SCN⁻/HSCN, N₃⁻ |
| Hydroxo / inorganic | OH⁻, HCO₃⁻, sulfide-related systems |
| N-donors | NH₃, ethylenediamine, pyridine, imidazole |
| Soft S/Se donors | Thiourea, thiosemicarbazide, selenourea, thiodiglycol |
| Chelators | EDTA, TDS |

At 25 °C, Hg(II) has especially strong representation for these systems:

| Hg(II) ligand | SRD‑46 VLM count | Beta definitions | Ionic strength range (M) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Hydroxide | 9 | 6 | 0–0.5 |
| Chloride | 8 | 4 | 0–0.5 |
| Thiourea | 8 | 5 | 0.1–0.5 |
| Iodide | 6 | 6 | 0.5 |
| Bromide | 5 | 5 | 0.5 |
| Cyanide / HCN | 4 | 4 | 0 |
| EDTA | 4 | 3 | 0.1–0.5 |
| Pyridine | 4 | 4 | 0.5 |
| Ethylenediamine | 3 | 3 | 0.1 |
| Thiocyanate / HSCN | 2 | 2 | 0 |
| Imidazole | 1 | 1 | 0.1 |
| Ammonia | 1 | 1 | 0.1 |

The **species patterns** available for Hg(II) are also broad: ML, ML₂, ML₃, ML₄, protonated complexes, hydroxo species, some polynuclear species, mixed chloro species, and solids. That is exactly what you’d expect for a **soft Lewis acid** with strong affinity for polarizable donors, especially sulfur and halides.

claims.text: The Hg(II) catalog includes halides, pseudohalides, hydroxide, sulfide-related ligands, neutral N-donors, soft S-donors, and chelators.

| Hydroxo / inorganic | OH⁻, HCO₃⁻, sulfide-related systems |
| Soft S/Se donors | Thiourea, thiosemicarbazide, selenourea, thiodiglycol |
At 25 °C, Hg(II) has especially strong representation for these systems:
The **species patterns** available for Hg(II) are also broad:
ML, ML₂, ML₃, ML₄, protonated complexes, hydroxo species, some polynuclear species, mixed chloro species, and solids.
That is exactly what you’d expect for a **soft Lewis acid**

paragraphs.text:
## Hg(I): narrower but still chemically interesting
SRD‑46 contains fewer Hg(I) systems. The most represented Hg(I) ligands in the retrieved set are simple inorganic anions and carboxylates.

| Hg(I) ligand | SRD‑46 VLM count | Beta definitions | Ionic strength range (M) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Acetate | 7 | 3 | 0–3 |
| Hydrogen sulfate / sulfate-related | 4 | 3 | 0–3 |
| Hydroxide | 4 | 3 | 0.5–3 |
| Phosphate | 3 | 2 | 0–3 |
| Nitrate | 3 | 2 | 0.5–3 |
| Chloride | 2 | 1 | 0–0.5 |
| Bromide | 2 | 1 | 0–0.5 |
| Iodide | 2 | 1 | 0–0.5 |
| Thiocyanate | 2 | 1 | 0–1 |
| Cyanide / HCN | 1 | 1 | 0 |

So, compared with Hg(II), **Hg(I) is less broadly represented** in the database and appears more often with simpler anionic ligands.

claims.text: ## Hg(I): narrower but still chemically interesting
The most represented Hg(I) ligands in the retrieved set are simple inorganic anions and carboxylates.
compared with Hg(II), **Hg(I) is less broadly represented** in the database
appears more often with simpler anionic ligands.

paragraphs.text:
## What stands out chemically
A few safe takeaways from the SRD‑46 coverage:

| Observation | Evidence from SRD‑46 |
|---|---|
| Hg(II) chemistry is much richer than Hg(I) | 20 Hg(II) systems and 92 species in the catalog vs a much smaller Hg(I) set |
| Soft-donor affinity is prominent for Hg(II) | strong coverage for thiourea, thiocyanate, cyanide, iodide, bromide, chloride |
| Hydrolysis matters | hydroxide is one of the most represented Hg(II) systems |
| Chelation is important but less dominant in coverage than soft monodentates | EDTA and ethylenediamine are present, but halides / hydroxide / thiourea have more entries |

claims.text: A few safe takeaways from the SRD‑46 coverage:
| Hg(II) chemistry is much richer than Hg(I) | 20 Hg(II) systems and 92 species in the catalog vs a much smaller Hg(I) set |
| Soft-donor affinity is prominent for Hg(II) | strong coverage for thiourea, thiocyanate, cyanide, iodide, bromide, chloride |
| Hydrolysis matters | hydroxide is one of the most represented Hg(II) systems |
| Chelation is important but less dominant in coverage than soft monodentates | EDTA and ethylenediamine are present, but halides / hydroxide / thiourea have more entries |

paragraphs.text:
## Short chemistry interpretation
Mercury follows classic **HSAB** behavior: **Hg(II)** strongly favors **soft donor atoms**, especially **S** and heavy halides, so it makes sense that thiourea, thiocyanate, iodide, bromide, and cyanide are all well represented. The appreciable hydroxide coverage also reflects that **aqueous speciation and hydrolysis** are important for mercury, especially outside strongly complexing media.

If you want, I can next pull **actual log β stability constants at ~25 °C** for a few hallmark systems such as **HgCl₄²⁻, HgI₄²⁻, Hg(CN)₄²⁻, Hg(thiourea)\_n²⁺, and Hg(EDTA)** from SRD‑46.

claims.text: thiourea, thiocyanate, iodide, bromide, and cyanide are all well represented.
**aqueous speciation and hydrolysis** are important for mercury
If you want, I can next pull
for a few hallmark systems such as
